ideas.title: ASHA Worker AI Risk Scorer
ideas.subtitle: India's 900,000 community health workers deserve AI tools built for them — not apps pointed at their patients
ideas.essay: Every Indian health AI startup builds apps for patients who have smartphones, internet, and health literacy — but the real bottleneck in community health is the ASHA worker herself, who visits 40-60 families a month with a paper register and no decision support. Stefan's India hospital rotations this week revealed exactly how ASHA workers operate: NIN Hyderabad food databases exist, the patients exist, the health system touchpoints exist — but there's no AI risk-scoring layer that tells the ASHA worker which family to prioritize tomorrow morning.
ideas.mechanism: Build with NIN Hyderabad food dataset + ASHA mobile platform (ASHA Soft) + Claude Haiku for real-time risk classification based on dietary intake + pregnancy status + child growth metrics; offline-first PWA with Devanagari language support
ideas.edge: Stefan's Hyderabad hospital rotation this week gave him direct observation of ASHA worker workflows that no Silicon Valley founder has — combined with NOURISH's Indian food dataset access and Dr. Mahari's Apollo connections for pilot deployment
seeds: OMI Conv 39 — Pragmatic Innovation in Healthcare Systems (Apr 12)
OMI Conv 13 — Nourish Team Strategy, Dr. Mahari + Apollo India network (Apr 19)
ideas.claudePrompt: Build an ASHA Worker AI Risk Scorer prototype. Context: India has 900,000 ASHA workers who visit 40-60 families monthly. They use paper registers with no digital decision support. Build: (1) a risk scoring algorithm that takes 5 inputs (family dietary diversity score, pregnancy status, child age under-5, last growth measurement, prior illness) and outputs 3-level risk (high/medium/low) with action recommendation, (2) a mobile-first PWA in React that an ASHA worker can use offline, (3) integrate NIN Hyderabad's food composition database for dietary scoring, (4) Devanagari/Hindi UI option. Use Claude Haiku API for the risk narrative generation. Target: ASHA worker sees risk level + 1-sentence action in under 10 seconds. Start with maternal and child nutrition module.

ideas.title: Last-Mile Translation Grant Positioner
ideas.subtitle: The literature knows what works — Stefan's competitive advantage is proving he can implement it
ideas.essay: Most nutrition grants fail because they propose to generate new knowledge that the systematic review literature already contains — reviewers know this, and they fund accordingly. The real funding gap is Last-Mile Translation: taking what's proven in RCTs and implementing it in culturally specific, underserved settings where nobody has bothered to test it. Stefan's April 16 grant strategy session crystallized this: NOURISH's real differentiator is not the AI, it's the credentials + real-world deployment (Apollo India + Nepal Ministry of Health + CGM South Asian cohort) that AI-generated applications cannot simulate.
ideas.mechanism: Build a grant positioning engine using Claude Sonnet API: takes a funder's priority areas + Stefan's existing deployments as inputs, outputs a Last-Mile Translation framing with specific evidence claims from NOURISH's real-world use cases; integrates with grant registry to auto-populate match scores
ideas.edge: Stefan's simultaneous presence in three healthcare systems (Stanford/US, Apollo/India, Nepal Ministry of Health) is the Last-Mile Translation credential that no other applicant can fabricate — and this week's OMI evidence makes clear he should lead with this in every LOI
seeds: OMI Conv 20 — Team Plans Grants, AI Support (Apr 16)
OMI Conv 40 — Pokhari Grant Proposal (Apr 12)

Output: a 3-paragraph LOI opening that positions NOURISH as Last-Mile Translation infrastructure, not new knowledge generation. Build as a simple React form + Claude API call. Pre-populate with Macy Foundation and PCORI as default funders.

ideas.title: India AMR EHR Trend Module
ideas.subtitle: Antimicrobial resistance kills 1.3M Indians annually — the EHR trend signal is there but invisible
ideas.essay: India has the world's highest AMR burden (1.3M deaths/year), yet most Indian hospital EHRs display antibiotic history as a flat list with no resistance trend visualization — a doctor prescribing a third-generation cephalosporin for a patient who has had it fail twice sees no system warning. Stefan's April 12 conversations (Conv 43 and 44) crystallized a concrete product: a low-friction EHR module that pulls a patient's antimicrobial history and renders a resistance trajectory — specifically designed for the fragmented Indian EHR ecosystem where lab data lives in separate systems.
ideas.mechanism: Build with HL7 FHIR R4 lab results API + a visual trend rendering layer (D3.js) + a Claude Haiku risk narrative generator; integrate with major Indian EMR systems (Practo, Medi EMR, eVital) via their existing REST APIs; AMR risk classification using WHO AWaRe categories
ideas.edge: Stefan's India hospital rotation this week gave him direct observation of AMR prescribing decisions in resource-constrained settings — NOURISH's India partnerships (Apollo, Nepal Ministry) provide pilot deployment channels that any US-based health IT company would need years to develop
seeds: OMI Conv 43 — Designing India-Specific EHR AMR Case Study (Apr 12)
OMI Conv 44 — Healthcare Tech Solutions And Lab Service Models (Apr 12)
ideas.claudePrompt: Build an AMR Trend EHR Module proof-of-concept for Indian hospital settings. The problem: a doctor prescribing antibiotics has no visual AMR trajectory for the patient. Build: (1) a React component that takes a patient's antibiotic history (list of drug name + date + outcome: effective/failed/partial) and renders a resistance trajectory chart using D3.js, (2) a risk classifier that flags High/Medium/Low AMR risk based on WHO AWaRe categories and prior resistance events, (3) a 1-sentence Claude Haiku narrative: 'This patient has failed 2 of 3 last antibiotic courses — consider culture before prescribing', (4) a demo with 3 synthetic patient cases (high AMR risk, medium, and low). Use a dark clinical theme. This module is designed to slot into existing Indian EMR systems (Practo-style) as a sidebar widget.

ideas.title: Sous Anti-Recipe Cooking Scaffold
ideas.subtitle: The reason every meal-planning app fails is that it gives people recipes — Sous gives them techniques
ideas.essay: Meal planning apps fail at exactly the moment they succeed: when users open the recipe library and confront 5,000 options, decision fatigue sets in, and they order delivery instead. Sous is explicitly designed around an anti-recipe architecture — one main + three pairings + one short cook flow — because the research on dietary behavior change shows that small repeated wins outperform high-complexity interventions by a 3:1 margin. Stefan's April 19 architecture conversation (Conv 13) revealed that the Sous differentiation is being undersold: the 'spice blooming' technique layer (Chef Andrew's method) is the South Asian equivalent of France's mise en place, and it's why Sous users can cook an unfamiliar cuisine in 20 minutes.
ideas.mechanism: Build with Sous food recognition engine (computer vision food ID) + spice blooming technique database + Claude Sonnet for culturally-aware ingredient pairing + an anchor-fit prompting pattern that scores all ingredient candidates against a fixed South Asian anchor spice; deploy as React Native + Expo for iOS/Android
ideas.edge: Stefan's QUAD Meal Pairer anchor-fit architecture and Chef Andrew's spice blooming methodology are NOURISH intellectual property that no competitor has — combined with the NOURISH CGM cohort providing the only South Asian dietary behavior dataset of this granularity in a Western research context
seeds: OMI Conv 13 — Nourish Team Strategy (Apr 19)
OMI Conv 39 — Pragmatic Innovation in Healthcare Systems (Apr 12)
ideas.claudePrompt: Build the Sous Anti-Recipe Cooking Scaffold concept into a functional prototype. Architecture: (1) an 'anchor-fit' ingredient scoring engine that takes 1 anchor spice (e.g., cumin) and ranks 50 other ingredients on cultural coherence + nutritional density + dietary compliance, (2) a 5-step cook flow generator that outputs: [technique name + visual cue + timing + sensory signal] rather than a recipe (e.g., 'bloom spices in oil: 30 seconds, smoke signal indicates ready'), (3) a Claude Sonnet prompt that takes dietary constraints + cultural background + available ingredients and generates the anchor + 3 pairings + cook flow. Build as a Next.js prototype with a clean mobile UI. Include the Chef Andrew spice blooming taxonomy: dry spices → wet spices → aromatics → protein → vegetables → liquid. No recipe list. No meal plan. Just the scaffold.

ideas.title: Hospital LOI Pipeline Generator
ideas.subtitle: The pre-seed check for a health startup requires exactly three documents — and none of them is a pitch deck
ideas.essay: Every health startup founder agonizes over the pitch deck, but the actual pre-seed check trigger ($100–300K) is a hospital Letter of Intent — which takes one conversation with a clinical department head and one paragraph on letterhead. Stefan's hackathon team learned this in real-time this week (Conv 36): Western Sydney Local Health District's CTI already gave informal approval, but that approval is worthless without a formal LOI, and the team has no process for converting informal approval into a signed document. The insight is generalizable: there are thousands of health startups with hospital supporters who have no idea how to operationalize that support into investor-grade evidence.
ideas.mechanism: Build with Claude Sonnet as the document generator + a 3-step wizard (problem statement → hospital stakeholder context → specific ask) + template library of hospital LOI formats from 5 health systems (WSLD, Stanford Health Care, VA, Kaiser, UCSF) + a 1-click email generator that drafts the outreach to the clinical department head
ideas.edge: Stefan's WSLD CTI approval this week is the proof-of-concept: he has lived the exact failure mode (informal approval → no LOI → investor credibility gap) and has direct relationships with WSLD clinical staff to pilot the LOI generator with a real hospital

OMI Conv 45 — Friends Debate Startups, Careers (Apr 12)
ideas.claudePrompt: Build a Hospital Letter of Intent Generator for health startups. Wizard flow: Step 1 — Startup problem statement (1 paragraph, plain language), Step 2 — Hospital stakeholder context (department, contact name, informal approval status, what they want in return), Step 3 — LOI specifics (pilot scope, data access needed, timeline, any payment). Output: (1) a 1-page formal LOI on hospital letterhead template (DOCX format), (2) an outreach email draft to the clinical department head to request the signed LOI, (3) a 3-bullet investor summary of what the LOI proves. Build as a Next.js app with a clean form UI. Pre-load Western Sydney Local Health District as the default hospital with realistic field examples. Ensure the LOI is investor-grade (specific scope, no vague language, signed by a named clinical officer).

ideas.title: Patient Story Video Library Infrastructure
ideas.subtitle: A $2,500 patient story video is grant infrastructure — not a production cost

ideas.claudePrompt: Build 'Nutrition HPI Builder' — a 6-question nutrition assessment that generates a clinical-note-ready addendum. 6 questions: (1) main protein sources this week, (2) main carbohydrate sources, (3) estimated meals per day, (4) main cooking method (fried/grilled/boiled/raw), (5) estimated fruit/vegetable servings per day, (6) any recent diet changes or restrictions. Claude Haiku generates a 3-sentence HPI addendum: 'Patient reports a [X]-type diet with [frequency] meals per day. Main protein sources include [Y]. Recent changes: [Z].' Doctor can copy-paste into clinical note. Next.js form, Tailwind, clean mobile UI.

ideas.name: Hackathon LOI Playbook
ideas.tagline: Win the hackathon, keep the prize — a step-by-step playbook for turning a win into pre-seed.
ideas.mechanic: Interactive checklist: hackathon win → 5 steps to fundable startup (LOI + patent + pilot + deck + investors); generates each document.
ideas.stack: Next.js + Claude Sonnet + progress tracker + Tailwind
ideas.researchAngle: What percentage of hackathon wins convert to funded startups, and what steps predict conversion? Publishable.
ideas.claudePrompt: Build 'Hackathon LOI Playbook' — a post-hackathon startup conversion guide. 5 interactive steps: Step 1: Hospital LOI (generate template), Step 2: Provisional Patent (generate claims outline, explain $2,000 filing process), Step 3: Pilot MOU (hospital memorandum of understanding template), Step 4: 1-Page Pitch Summary for investors, Step 5: Pre-seed ask amount calculator (based on LOI count × $100K base). Each step has: description + why it matters + Claude-generated document draft + download button. Progress bar at top. Next.js + Tailwind. Include the WSLD Western Sydney Local Health District as a worked example.

ideas.name: PFME Session Designer
ideas.tagline: Design a 3-hour resident nutrition workshop — with kitchen time, games, and two measurable objectives.
ideas.mechanic: Input: patient population + dietary focus + session length → output: full agenda + facilitator guide + kitchen activity plan.
ideas.stack: Next.js + Claude Sonnet + DOCX export (docx npm) + Tailwind
ideas.researchAngle: Do resident nutrition sessions designed by AI-assisted curricula achieve better learning outcomes? IRB-approvable study.
ideas.claudePrompt: Build 'PFME Session Designer' — a curriculum design tool for resident nutrition education. Inputs: patient population (South Asian / South American / general), dietary focus (T2D / weight loss / general wellness), session length (2 / 3 / 4 hours), has cooking facilities (yes/no). Output via Claude Sonnet: (1) 2 behavioral objectives written in observable terms, (2) full session agenda with time blocks (intro, cases, kitchen activity, role-play, debrief), (3) 3 kitchen activities with hands-on instructions, (4) facilitator guide with key teaching points, (5) post-session survey (5 questions). Download as DOCX. Next.js + Tailwind.

ideas.name: Sprintax Buddy
ideas.tagline: F-1 OPT student taxes are a nightmare — this explains every line in plain English.
ideas.mechanic: Upload W-2 or 1042-S → extract key fields → explain each line in plain English → output a checklist of next steps.
ideas.stack: Next.js + Claude Sonnet for explanation + PDF parsing (pdf.js) + Tailwind
ideas.researchAngle: What tax errors are most common among international STEM students? IRS training set opportunity.
ideas.claudePrompt: Build 'Sprintax Buddy' — a tax explainer for F-1 OPT international students. Upload a W-2 or 1042-S (PDF or image). Use pdf.js to extract text, send to Claude Sonnet with the prompt: 'You are a tax advisor for F-1 OPT international students. Explain each field of this tax document in plain English. Identify any amounts that need to be entered in Sprintax. Flag any fields that are unusual for an international student.' Output: field-by-field explanation + Sprintax entry guide + 3-step checklist. No financial advice disclaimer included in UI. Next.js + Tailwind.

ideas.name: Ward Round Recorder
ideas.tagline: Capture ward round decisions in real-time — sync to patient notes without typing.
ideas.mechanic: Tap 'start round' → voice capture per patient → Claude extracts plan, medications, follow-up → paste to EMR.
ideas.stack: Next.js + Web Speech API + Claude Sonnet for structuring + Clipboard API
ideas.researchAngle: Does structured voice capture reduce ward round documentation time? Publishable in medical informatics.
ideas.claudePrompt: Build 'Ward Round Recorder' — a voice-to-clinical-note app. Flow: doctor taps 'New Patient' → dictates ward round findings for 30-60 seconds → Claude Sonnet structures the note into: (1) Impression (1 sentence), (2) Plan (bulleted), (3) Medications changed (if any), (4) Follow-up (date + responsible). Doctor taps 'Copy to EMR' → formatted note in clipboard. Build with Web Speech API for browser-based voice capture. Support 10 simultaneous patients in a session. Next.js. Clinical dark theme.

ideas.name: Research Poster Critic
ideas.tagline: Paste your poster abstract — get the 5 questions a reviewer will ask, with specific rewrites.
ideas.mechanic: Paste abstract + methods + results → Claude returns 5 critical reviewer questions + specific rewrite suggestions.
ideas.stack: Next.js + Claude Sonnet + Tailwind
ideas.researchAngle: Does AI-generated reviewer feedback improve abstract acceptance rates? Publishable with pre/post data.
ideas.claudePrompt: Build 'Research Poster Critic' — an academic abstract reviewer. User pastes: title + abstract + methods + results + conclusion. Claude Sonnet returns: (1) 5 specific reviewer questions the poster will face, (2) for each question: the weakness in the current text that prompts it, (3) a specific rewrite (1-3 sentences) that addresses the weakness, (4) an overall rating: Accept / Minor Revision / Major Revision, (5) top 3 strengths to keep. Single-page Next.js app. Dark theme. Copy-to-clipboard for each rewrite suggestion.

ideas.name: Hackathon Deck Builder
ideas.tagline: Build a 5-slide hackathon pitch deck in 20 minutes — problem, solution, evidence, team, ask.
ideas.mechanic: Fill 5 structured inputs → Claude generates slide content → export as presentation outline + talking points.
ideas.stack: Next.js + Claude Sonnet + Tailwind + html2canvas for PDF export
ideas.researchAngle: Which slide structures predict hackathon wins? Analyze 100 winning decks — publishable.
ideas.claudePrompt: Build 'Hackathon Deck Builder' — a 5-slide pitch deck generator. 5 inputs: (1) Problem: who suffers + how badly, (2) Solution: what you built + how it works in 1 sentence, (3) Evidence: what validation you have (LOI, pilot, usage), (4) Team: who you are and why you, (5) Ask: what you need (investment, partnership, users). Claude Sonnet generates: slide title + 3 bullet points + presenter talking points for each slide. Display as 5 styled cards. Export button generates a printable HTML version via html2canvas. Next.js + Tailwind. Include WSLD hackathon team as a worked example.

ideas.name: PGx Food Scanner
ideas.tagline: Scan a food label — see if the flavors match your pharmacogenomics profile.
ideas.mechanic: Upload food label photo → Claude Vision extracts ingredients → cross-reference CYP450 flavor interactions → flag matches.
ideas.stack: Next.js + Claude Vision API + static CYP450 flavor compound database + Tailwind
ideas.researchAngle: Does PGx-aware food labeling change dietary choices? Publishable in pharmacogenomics + nutrition intersection.
ideas.claudePrompt: Build 'PGx Food Scanner' — a pharmacogenomics-aware food label analyzer. Step 1: user uploads food label photo. Claude Vision extracts ingredient list. Step 2: cross-reference each ingredient against a static CYP450 flavor compound database: CYP1A2 (caffeine, cruciferous vegetables), CYP2D6 (tyramine, capsaicin), TAS2R38 (bitter compounds). Step 3: output a PGx flag panel: Green = compatible with typical profile, Yellow = check your CYP variant, Red = strong interaction if you're a slow metabolizer. Include a demo mode with 3 pre-loaded food labels. Next.js + Tailwind + Claude Vision API.

ideas.name: Sous CGM Explainer
ideas.tagline: Your CGM spike pattern explained in plain English — and what to cook tonight to fix it.
ideas.mechanic: Upload CGM graph image → Claude Vision identifies spike pattern → suggests 3 Sous meal modifications.
ideas.stack: Next.js + Claude Vision API + Sous recipe database stub + Tailwind
ideas.researchAngle: Does personalized CGM-to-meal feedback improve glucose outcomes vs. generic advice? CGM trial add-on.
ideas.claudePrompt: Build 'Sous CGM Explainer' — a CGM graph interpreter for patients. Upload a CGM graph image. Claude Vision analyzes and returns: (1) spike pattern classification (post-meal spike / overnight drift / dawn phenomenon / flat control), (2) plain English explanation of what the pattern means (1 paragraph, zero jargon), (3) 3 specific Sous meal modifications to try this week to address the pattern (e.g., 'Try dal before rice for your next South Asian meal to flatten the post-lunch spike'), (4) 1 question to bring to your doctor. Single-page Next.js app. Warm dark theme. Mobile-optimized for patients to use at home.

ideas.name: VR ADL Score
ideas.tagline: Score activities of daily living across 4 domains — the ShopWell VR framework, generalized.
ideas.mechanic: Select patient condition + ADL category → generate difficulty progression (16 levels) across cognitive/physical/visual/environmental.
ideas.stack: Next.js + Claude Sonnet for level design + Tailwind table UI
ideas.researchAngle: Does a 4-domain ADL difficulty framework improve VR rehabilitation outcomes vs. single-domain? Publishable.
ideas.claudePrompt: Build 'VR ADL Score' — a VR rehabilitation level design tool based on the ShopWell hemianopia framework. Inputs: patient condition (hemianopia / post-stroke dominant hand / spatial neglect / processing speed deficit), ADL target (grocery shopping / cooking / navigation / workplace task). Output: a 16-level difficulty matrix across 4 domains: Cognitive (decision complexity), Physical (motor demand), Visual (field requirement), Environmental (scene complexity). Each cell: brief level description + measurable success criterion. Claude Sonnet generates the matrix. Display as a 16×4 table. Export as CSV. Next.js + Tailwind.

ideas.name: Spice Blooming Timer
ideas.tagline: A cooking timer that teaches technique — not just counts down.
ideas.mechanic: Select spice combination → get stage-by-stage visual cue guide + timers with sensory signals for each stage.
ideas.stack: Next.js + Web Audio API for stage alerts + Tailwind
ideas.researchAngle: Does technique-cued cooking guidance produce better dietary adherence than recipe compliance? Publishable.
ideas.claudePrompt: Build 'Spice Blooming Timer' — a stage-based cooking guide for South Asian spice techniques. UI: dropdown of 20 spice combinations (cumin + coriander / black mustard + curry leaf / cardamom + cinnamon / etc.). Each combination shows 4 stages with: stage name, duration (seconds), visual cue to watch for, sound/smell signal, audio alert when done. Stages: dry spice bloom → aroma release → wet spice integration → aromatics. Web Audio API chime between stages. Large clear text for kitchen use. PWA so it works without internet. Next.js + Tailwind.

ideas.name: Grant Deadline Calendar
ideas.tagline: Import your grant registry — get a Gantt chart with real action deadlines, not just submission dates.
ideas.mechanic: Paste grant CSV or JSON → Claude extracts real action windows → output a Gantt chart with 14/7/2-day warning bands.
ideas.stack: Next.js + Claude Sonnet + D3.js Gantt + Tailwind
ideas.researchAngle: Do researchers who see 'real action window' deadlines vs. submission deadlines submit more grants? Study design.
ideas.claudePrompt: Build 'Grant Deadline Calendar' — a Gantt chart generator for grant management. Input: paste grant data as JSON array [{name, funder, submission_deadline, loi_deadline, nomination_gate, amount, score}]. Claude Sonnet identifies the real action window for each grant (nomination gates, LOI deadlines, internal review time). D3.js renders a Gantt chart with: submission date (solid bar), LOI deadline (dashed bar), nomination gate (red dot if ≤ 14 days from today), 14/7/2-day warning bands in red/orange/yellow. Click a grant to expand details. Export as PDF. Next.js + Tailwind.

candidates.name: OMI → Optimus Commitment Loop
candidates.pattern: Stefan feeds OMI audio to Claude daily and pays $300/month — but the behavioral feedback loop from OMI back to his task queue is still manual. omi-commitment-capture skill exists but the review queue loop is incomplete.

candidates.name: Razer Proposal OTL Tracker
candidates.pattern: The Razer partnership requires OTL review → Rich sign-off → Dr. Mahari commitment. Three sequential approvals with no tracking. An automated status tracker with email nudges at each stage would prevent the approval chain from stalling.

candidates.name: PFME Session Auto-Agenda
candidates.pattern: PFME curriculum requires a 3-hour session design every iteration. The 4-domain framework (objectives + activities + kitchen + debrief) is fully templatable. A Claude-powered form that generates the full agenda + facilitator guide in 5 minutes replaces hours of manual design.

candidates.name: Grant Real-Deadline Alerter
candidates.pattern: Hidden nomination gates (ADA GR-071 nominal gate ≈ 2 weeks before public deadline) are a systematic failure mode. Automated 14/7/2-day push alerts for the REAL action deadline (not submission date) would have caught 3 near-misses this week.

candidates.name: REDCap Instrument Builder
candidates.pattern: REDCap instrument creation involves: branching logic design → CSV data dictionary → import → QA. This is fully automatable: provide natural language survey description → Claude generates the data dictionary CSV with correct branching_logic column → ready to import.

candidates.name: Grant LOI Auto-Drafter
candidates.pattern: Each hospital LOI requires the same 5 structural components (problem framing, Stefan asset list, specific ask, timeline, contact) that can be templated from a 30-min OMI briefing into a near-final DOCX.

candidates.name: OMI to Airtable Commitment Sync
candidates.pattern: Stefan makes verbal commitments in OMI-recorded meetings that never reach Airtable. Claude extracts 'I will X by Y' patterns from transcripts and auto-creates tasks, closing the verbal-to-written gap.

candidates.name: CGM Participant Report Generator
candidates.pattern: NOURISH CGM participants receive form letters; personalized plain-language glucose interpretation reports would double as recruitment material. Claude converts CGM CSV to PDF.

candidates.name: PFME Session Designer
candidates.pattern: PFME curriculum sessions require Bloom's taxonomy alignment, behavioral objective mapping, and teaching kitchen logistics. Claude generates a full session plan from a 15-min briefing transcript.

candidates.name: Grant Deadline Calendar Sync
candidates.pattern: Grant registry has 74+ entries with due dates. A weekly Claude script extracts upcoming deadlines, writes to Obsidian calendar, and sends a Slack digest every Monday morning.

candidates.name: Collaborator Brief Generator
candidates.pattern: Before every Lata/Malti/Dr. Mahari meeting, Stefan manually assembles context. Claude auto-generates a 1-page collaborator brief from the entity page + recent OMI mentions + open action items.

candidates.name: Research Poster Critic
candidates.pattern: Stefan submits research posters to conferences and gets feedback too late. Claude reviews poster PDF against conference rubric (visual hierarchy, methods, results) and returns structured critique in 5 min.

candidates.name: Sous Nutritional Data Enricher
candidates.pattern: Sous recipes lack nutritional data. Claude calls USDA FoodData Central API per ingredient, aggregates macros/micros, and writes back structured nutritional JSON to each recipe automatically.

candidates.name: Clinical SOAP Formatter
candidates.pattern: Ward round notes are dictated into OMI in free-form. Claude extracts structured SOAP format from the transcript, ready to paste into the EMR or email to the clinical team.

crossSourcePatterns: Stefan's OMI corpus is the richest behavioral data source in his system — yet the OMI → action pipeline is still the weakest link (manual review, no automation)
Three pending grant actions require parallel stakeholder management (Razer/OTL/Rich, Lata+Malti, Pokhari) — none has an automated status tracker
The PFME curriculum decision (video vs. live) is blocked by cost analysis that should take 30 minutes but hasn't been done — a simple spreadsheet would unblock it
Hackathon team has hospital approval but no weekly meeting — the win converts to nothing without team infrastructure
